three_body.instability_note: Moderate conflict between the three signals (46-pt spread). sector peer rank reads better than FTSE 100 position implies. The FTSE 100's overweight in energy and mining inflates relative ESG rankings.
three_body.resolution: Our resolution: Integrity 84/100 × WEM 52/100 → 'Preferred Sustainability Leader'. Jangani, Date & Tucker (SSRN 5618192, 2026) show driver rankings shift across regime windows (COVID → COP26 → energy crisis). We anchor to verifiable harm metrics (emissions, pay ratios, enforcement records) that are regime-stable — not ESG labels that oscillate with market narrative.

summary_investor: Unilever plc is projected to hold steady in its consumer peer ranking (1→1 of 2) over 3 months. FTSE 100 position: 54th→56th percentile. WEM 52/100 — moderate harm exposure — hold, engage on material drivers. Placebo Index 0.68 — ESG narrative overstates real performance.
summary_corporate: 12-month sector ranking forecast: hold steady (1→1 of 2). FTSE 100: 54th→58th percentile. Highest-leverage action: Physical Impacts of Climate Change (74% materiality) — this single driver has the greatest impact on FTSE 100 ranking improvement.
summary_auditor: Of 2 ESG claims analysed, 1 (50%) are independently verifiable. No directly contradicted claims — disclosure quality above sector average. ESG provider spread: 6 pts — within normal range. Priority: third-party audit of Physical Impacts of Climate Change measurement methodology.
crowd_consensus: Aggregated signal across investor, corporate, and audit lenses: Stable — hold; monitor material driver trajectory. Driver rankings are temporally unstable across market regimes (Jangani et al., SSRN 5618192). The 3-body instability — ESG score, peer rank, FTSE position pulling in different directions — is resolved by WEM (52/100) × Integrity (84/100): regime-stable signals grounded in verifiable harm, not rater methodology.
